serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of paneer butter masala with jeera rice is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 150 g of paneer butter masala sauce with paneer cubes and 200 g of jeera rice.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer Butter Masala with Jeera Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010521 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record; estimated at 2.1 g/100g based on ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for a comparable paneer butter masala and basmati rice combination, documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for paneer butter masala with jeera rice as a combined dish. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on basmati rice GI of 58 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) as the dominant carbohydrate contributor, moderated downward slightly by the protein and fat content of the paneer butter masala component. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (56) × carbohydrate per serving (44.4 g for 350 g serving) / 100 = 24.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio < 1.0) and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, which represents 35–40% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertension management; patients should request reduced salt and reduced butter/ghee preparation and monitor cumulative sodium intake across the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.2 g/100g with medium GI (~56); dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g and paneer protein moderate postprandial glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: A standard 350 g serving provides approximately 88 g carbohydrate and a glycaemic load of ~25, which is at the upper boundary of the medium GL range; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter, ghee, and cream (~2.1 g/100g estimated); sodium 231 mg/100g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g in HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from butter and ghee in the masala sauce may contribute to LDL elevation with regular consumption; patients with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with reduced butter and ghee, substitute cream with low-fat yogurt, and keep serving size to 200–250 g to limit saturated fat intake to below 5 g per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Butter and ghee (pro-inflammatory saturated fats); nightshade ingredients (tomato, red chili powder) may trigger joint sensitivity in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the combination of animal-derived saturated fats (butter, ghee, cream) and nightshade ingredients (tomato, red chili powder); if consumed, request substitution of butter and ghee with extra-virgin olive oil and reduction or elimination of nightshade components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Butter Masala with Jeera Rice is a popular North Indian vegetarian dish served widely in Indian restaurants across Singapore and globally. The dish consists of two components: paneer butter masala, a rich tomato-based curry made with cubes of fresh Indian cottage cheese (paneer) simmered in a velvety sauce of butter, ghee, onion, tomato, cashew paste, cream, ginger, garlic, and aromatic spices including garam masala and red chili powder; and jeera rice, fragrant basmati rice tempered with cumin seeds (jeera), bay leaf, and ghee. The combined dish delivers a creamy, mildly spiced, slightly tangy flavour profile with a soft, tender texture from the paneer and fluffy, aromatic rice. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 56 (medium) based on basmati rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, moderated by the protein and fat from the masala component. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but requires caution for individuals managing hypertension (sodium load ~809 mg per 350 g serving), type 2 diabetes (medium GL ~25 per serving), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from butter and ghee). It is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to pro-inflammatory saturated fats and nightshade ingredients. The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible, making it broadly accessible in Singapore's multicultural food landscape.

GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this combined dish. 5. The input food name 'Paneer Butter Masala ❤️ Free Jeera Rice' has been normalised to 'Paneer Butter Masala with Jeera Rice' as the canonical English food name; the original name is preserved in aliases. 6.
